title: All-Equity ETF Showdown: XEQT vs VEQT vs 10 Alternatives — Which One Should You Buy in 2026?
description: We compared 12 all-equity and equity-focused ETFs available to Canadian investors. XEQT (iShares) and VEQT (Vanguard) are the two heavyweights — both offer global diversification, low fees, and strong recent returns. XEQT wins on track record and size, but VEQT just got a fee cut and offers better value going forward. For most people starting out, either one is a solid choice. For the specific situation (like building in an RRSP), ITOT or IEFA might be smarter.
verdict:
**Score: 9/10.**

VEQT is your answer for 2026. The November 2025 fee cut from 0.22% to 0.17% makes it the cheapest major all-equity ETF going forward (MER ~0.19–0.20% vs XEQT's 0.20%). It holds 13,000 stocks, returned 21.77% in 1 year, and has C$12.2B in rock-solid AUM. XEQT is a close second and slightly larger, but VEQT's new fee structure wins on value.

**Would you tell your friend to buy VEQT?** Yes. If they're opening a TFSA or RRSP and want one simple, globally diversified fund, VEQT is the easiest answer. No need to pick individual stocks, rebalance, or think about currency. Set it and forget it. XEQT is equally good if they prefer iShares, but VEQT's cost advantage tips the scales.

**Not suitable if:** You live outside Canada (FX complexity), want emerging market overexposure (buy IEMG separately), or prefer leverage (buy HEQL—but really, don't as a beginner). Otherwise, VEQT is the no-brainer.
tags: Best All-Equity ETF for Canadians 2026: XEQT vs VEQT vs 10 Alternatives

content.headline: All-Equity ETF Showdown: XEQT vs VEQT vs 10 Alternatives — Which One Should You Buy in 2026?
content.summary: We compared 12 all-equity and equity-focused ETFs available to Canadian investors. XEQT (iShares) and VEQT (Vanguard) are the two heavyweights — both offer global diversification, low fees, and strong recent returns. XEQT wins on track record and size, but VEQT just got a fee cut and offers better value going forward. For most people starting out, either one is a solid choice. For the specific situation (like building in an RRSP), ITOT or IEFA might be smarter.

candidates.reasonConsidered: VEQT just cut its management fee from 0.22% to 0.17% (Nov 18, 2025), and the MER will drop to ~0.19–0.20% at fiscal year-end—making it cheaper than XEQT going forward. It returned 21.77% in 1 year, has C$12.2B in AUM, and holds ~13,000 stocks across 4 regions. Annual distributions keep tax drag lower in non-registered accounts.

XEQT is the largest all-equity ETF in Canada (C$14.8B AUM) with a proven track record since 2019. It returned 22.07% in 1 year, has a low 0.20% MER, and offers quarterly distributions. Beta of 1.00 means it moves with the market—exactly as expected for a diversified global fund.
candidates.reasonEliminated: VEQT's recent fee cut and higher number of holdings (13,000 vs 9,000) give it a slight edge on value and diversification going forward, even though XEQT is slightly larger and has a better recent 1-year return.

candidates.reasonConsidered: ZEQT offers a unique feature: dedicated mid-cap (ZMID) and small-cap (ZSML) US sleeves (3.73% combined) that most peers skip. It returned 20.47% in 1 year, has a 0.20% MER (matching XEQT), and trades at C$20.57 with quarterly distributions.
candidates.reasonEliminated: At C$450.6M AUM, ZEQT is 30x smaller than XEQT and 27x smaller than VEQT, raising liquidity and closure concerns. The mid/small-cap tilt is interesting but adds complexity—beginners don't need it.

candidates.company: Global X All-Equity Asset Allocation ETF
candidates.reasonConsidered: HEQT overweights US tech and growth (55% US vs 45% in XEQT, with Nasdaq-100 overweight) and returned 19.74% in 1 year. It trades at C$22.14 with a lower P/E of 18.34 vs XEQT's 21.43, suggesting less expensive positioning.
candidates.reasonEliminated: HEQT uses a swap-based structure that can trigger phantom taxable distributions in non-registered accounts—a real tax headache. Smaller AUM (C$322M) and a growth tilt that may underperform in a value rotation. Beginner-unfriendly.

candidates.reasonConsidered: FEQT has a structural 0% management fee (Fidelity waives it permanently) and returned 18.56% in 1 year. It includes a 2.4% Bitcoin allocation for crypto exposure and applies factor tilts (low volatility, value, momentum) to tilt away from pure cap-weighted returns.
candidates.reasonEliminated: The 0.39% MER is 2x XEQT's—a significant drag over time. Factor tilts are unproven and may underperform simple cap-weighted funds. The Bitcoin allocation adds unpredictable volatility. This is too complex for someone just starting out.

candidates.company: Global X Enhanced All-Equity Asset Allocation ETF
candidates.reasonConsidered: HEQL is a 1.25x leveraged version of HEQT—it borrows money to amplify returns. It returned 22.39% in 1 year, beating XEQT and VEQT.
candidates.reasonEliminated: AUM is only C$9.5M—dangerously small. Real risk the fund closes and forces you to sell. 1.84% MER (including leverage costs) is very expensive. Leverage means losses are also amplified. Never use leverage as a beginner.

candidates.reasonConsidered: MIX is brand new (Apr 2025) with a 1.25x leverage component. It holds US stocks, US Treasuries, and gold—not a pure equity fund. Management fee waiver expires Apr 30, 2026.
candidates.reasonEliminated: MIX is NOT an all-equity ETF (holds bonds and gold). Brand new with only C$18M AUM and waived fees expiring in 1 year. Too risky and doesn't fit the "all-equity" brief. Avoid.

candidates.company: iShares Core MSCI All Country World ex Canada Index ETF
candidates.reasonConsidered: XAW offers 100% exposure to global stocks excluding Canada (65% US, 23% intl developed, 12% EM) with a 0.22% MER and returned 15.96% in 1 year. It's meant to pair with XIC (Canadian equities) for a DIY global portfolio.
candidates.reasonEliminated: On its own, XAW gives you zero Canada exposure—you'd need to pair it with XIC to replicate what XEQT/VEQT do automatically. That adds complexity. For a beginner, an all-in-one ETF beats a 2-fund portfolio every time.

candidates.company: iShares Core S&P/TSX Capped Composite Index ETF
candidates.reasonConsidered: XIC is the cheapest TSX ETF with a 0.06% MER and C$26.3B AUM. It returned ~38% in 1 year (Canadian equities had a great run). Dividends get full Canadian dividend tax credit outside a TFSA.
candidates.reasonEliminated: XIC is 100% Canadian—it doesn't give you global diversification. Alone, it's over-weighted to financials (~35%) and energy (~15%), which is Canada-specific risk. A beginner needs global diversification, not just Canada.

candidates.company: iShares Core S&P Total U.S. Stock Market ETF
candidates.reasonConsidered: ITOT is a US-listed ETF (in USD) with an incredibly low 0.03% MER and US$82.4B AUM. It holds 3,400+ US stocks and returned 18.2% in 1 year. In an RRSP, you avoid the 15% US withholding tax via the Canada-US treaty.
candidates.reasonEliminated: ITOT is US-only—you'd need to combine it with IEFA (international) and add Canadian exposure separately. For a beginner, this multi-fund approach is overkill. And it's USD-denominated, so you face currency risk and conversion costs.

candidates.reasonConsidered: IEFA covers developed international markets (Europe, Asia) with US$171.4B AUM and a 0.07% MER. It returned 21.07% in 1 year and pays a higher yield (~3.45%) than US-focused funds. Morningstar Bronze Medalist.
candidates.reasonEliminated: IEFA is a specialist play—developed international only. You'd need ITOT + IEMG + Canadian exposure to build a global portfolio. Too fragmented for beginners. A single all-in-one ETF (XEQT/VEQT) is simpler and equally good.

candidates.company: iShares Core MSCI Emerging Markets ETF
candidates.reasonConsidered: IEMG offers the hottest returns: 33–38% in 1 year and 18.95% annualized over 3 years. It includes South Korea (a key omission in competitors). US$135.5B AUM and a low 0.09% MER. Morningstar Bronze Medalist.
candidates.reasonEliminated: IEMG is emerging markets only—you'd need to pair it with ITOT and IEFA to get full diversification. Emerging markets are volatile and suitable only for intermediate investors. XEQT/VEQT already give you 5–7% EM exposure with less risk.

winner.reasonConsidered: VEQT just cut its management fee to 0.17% on November 18, 2025—the lowest among major all-equity ETFs. The MER will drop to ~0.19–0.20% at fiscal year-end, beating XEQT's 0.20%. It holds ~13,000 stocks (vs XEQT's ~9,000), giving you slightly better diversification. VEQT returned 21.77% in 1 year, has C$12.2B AUM, and pays annual distributions (simpler tax reporting). For a Canadian investor building long-term wealth in a TFSA or RRSP, VEQT offers the best value today and going forward.

content.analysis:
## Head-to-Head

| ETF | MER | AUM (CAD) | 1Y Return | Price | Type | Best For |
|-----|-----|-----------|-----------|-------|------|----------|
| **VEQT** | 0.24%→~0.19% | C$12.2B | 21.77% | C$54.60 | All-Equity Global | **Winner: lowest future cost** |
| XEQT | 0.20% | C$14.8B | 22.07% | C$40.59 | All-Equity Global | Largest, proven track record |
| ZEQT | 0.20% | C$450M | 20.47% | C$20.57 | All-Equity + US mid/small-cap | Unique feature, tiny fund |
| HEQT | ~0.17% | C$322M | 19.74% | C$22.14 | Growth-tilted all-equity | Tech overweight, tax traps |
| FEQT | 0.39% | C$4.1B | 18.56% | C$16.98 | Factor-tilted + crypto | Expensive, complex |
| HEQL | 1.84% | C$9.5M | 22.39% | ~C$11 | 1.25x leveraged | **AVOID: closure risk** |
| MIX | 0%→0.35% | C$18M | N/A | C$20.39 | Leveraged multi-asset | **AVOID: not all-equity, brand new** |
| XAW | 0.22% | C$3.8B | 15.96% | C$50.70 | Global ex-Canada | Need to pair with XIC |
| XIC | 0.06% | C$26.3B | ~38% | C$54.06 | Canadian only | Only if you want Canada-only |
| ITOT | 0.03% | US$82B | 18.2% | US$146.86 | US market only | DIY builders, RRSP focused |
| IEFA | 0.07% | US$171B | 21.07% | US$91.42 | Dev intl only | Specialist play, not standalone |
| IEMG | 0.09% | US$135B+ | 33–38% | US$69.75 | Emerging mkts only | Hot returns, high volatility |

**Plain English Notes:**
- **MER (Management Expense Ratio)**: The annual cost you pay to own the fund—like a fee for the fund manager's work. Lower is better. VEQT's 0.24% is about to drop to 0.19%, making it the cheapest big-name all-equity fund.
- **AUM (Assets Under Management)**: How much money is in the fund. Bigger = safer (less closure risk). XEQT is larger, but VEQT is still massive at C$12.2B.
- **1-Year Return**: How much the fund gained in the past 12 months. XEQT leads, but past returns don't predict future ones. VEQT is close behind.
- **Price**: What one unit costs today. Doesn't matter much—focus on the fees and returns instead.

## Why VEQT Wins

VEQT just got a fee cut. On November 18, 2025, Vanguard cut the management fee from 0.22% to 0.17%. That means by the end of fiscal 2026, the total MER will drop from 0.24% to roughly 0.19–0.20%—matching or beating XEQT's 0.20%. **Over 30 years, a 0.05% fee difference saves you thousands of dollars.**

VEQT also holds more stocks (~13,000) than XEQT (~9,000), meaning better diversification. It paid only annual distributions (vs XEQT's quarterly), which is cleaner for tax reporting. And it returned 21.77% in the past year—nearly identical to XEQT's 22.07%, confirming they perform similarly.

For a beginner or someone building a TFSA/RRSP, VEQT is the no-brainer choice today. You get global diversification, rock-bottom fees, and Vanguard's reputation for keeping costs low.

## The Runner-Up

XEQT is still excellent and the current market leader by AUM (C$14.8B). It returned 22.07% in 1 year, has a proven track record since August 2019, and a 0.20% MER is very competitive. iShares' Morningstar Medalist rating shows it's a quality fund. If you prefer slightly lower current fees or iShares' brand, XEQT works fine. But VEQT's fee cut tips the scale—in 2026, you're paying less for a fund with more holdings. That's a win.

**For US/International DIY Builders:** If you're comfortable managing multiple ETFs in an RRSP, ITOT (0.03% MER, US$82B) is absurdly cheap for US exposure, and IEFA (0.07%, US$171B) covers developed international. You'd add XIC (0.06%) for Canada. This costs slightly less long-term but requires active rebalancing and suits only experienced investors.

**For Emerging Market Upside:** IEMG returned 33–38% in 1 year—eye-popping. But this is a specialist play for investors comfortable with volatility. It's not suitable as a sole holding.

**Funds to Avoid:**
- **HEQL & MIX:** Dangerously small AUM (C$9.5M and C$18M). Real closure risk. Leverage amplifies losses. Beginners: stay away.
- **FEQT:** 0.39% MER is 2x XEQT. The 2.4% Bitcoin allocation and factor tilts add unneeded complexity and cost.
- **HEQT:** Swap-based structure triggers phantom distributions in taxable accounts. Growth tilt may underperform. Skip it.
risks: VEQT's MER will not drop to 0.19% until fiscal year-end (likely June 2026)—until then, it's still 0.24%, identical to XEQT. Don't assume the fee cut is live yet.
All-equity ETFs (XEQT, VEQT, ZEQT) suffer embedded Level 2 US withholding tax on dividends even in a TFSA—you can't recover this. It's built into returns, but it's real.
ZEQT, HEQT, and HEQL have much smaller AUM than XEQT/VEQT—closure or forced liquidation risk is real for funds under C$500M.
Emerging markets (IEMG) and growth-heavy funds (HEQT) are volatile—a 20–30% decline in a downturn is possible.
MIX just launched (Apr 2025) with only C$18M AUM and a 0% fee waiver ending Apr 30, 2026—not suitable for long-term investing.
US-listed ETFs (ITOT, IEFA, IEMG) are USD-denominated and expose you to currency risk; use them primarily in an RRSP where the fee structure favors them.
catalysts: VEQT's MER will officially drop to ~0.19–0.20% at fiscal year-end 2026, locking in the fee-cut advantage vs XEQT—this is when VEQT's value case becomes crystal clear.
US equity returns have led in 2024–2025; if international developed or emerging markets outperform in 2026–2027, XEQT/VEQT's broad global tilt will benefit.
Interest rate cuts by the Bank of Canada could push equities higher—all-equity funds benefit directly, while bond-heavy portfolios lag.
Canadian bank stocks and energy names (large TSX holdings) could rebound if commodity prices stabilize, boosting returns for these globally diversified ETFs.
Bitcoin volatility (FEQT holds 2.4%) could inflate or deflate returns unpredictably, making FEQT more/less attractive to crypto-curious investors.

dataPoints.label: VEQT MER (current)
dataPoints.value: 0.24% (expected to drop to ~0.19–0.20% at fiscal year-end 2026)

content.finalVerdict:
**Score: 9/10.**

VEQT is your answer for 2026. The November 2025 fee cut from 0.22% to 0.17% makes it the cheapest major all-equity ETF going forward (MER ~0.19–0.20% vs XEQT's 0.20%). It holds 13,000 stocks, returned 21.77% in 1 year, and has C$12.2B in rock-solid AUM. XEQT is a close second and slightly larger, but VEQT's new fee structure wins on value.

**Would you tell your friend to buy VEQT?** Yes. If they're opening a TFSA or RRSP and want one simple, globally diversified fund, VEQT is the easiest answer. No need to pick individual stocks, rebalance, or think about currency. Set it and forget it. XEQT is equally good if they prefer iShares, but VEQT's cost advantage tips the scales.

**Not suitable if:** You live outside Canada (FX complexity), want emerging market overexposure (buy IEMG separately), or prefer leverage (buy HEQL—but really, don't as a beginner). Otherwise, VEQT is the no-brainer.
